speaker: interviewer
text: The date is Tuesday, January the 30th, 1996. The liberator is the Reverend Dr. Isaac Levy. Interviewer is Sharon Tyler. City is London, in England. And the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Am I to go in?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll follow you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got to go and stand.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, OK, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't put me off. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep, rolling now.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Tuesday the 30th of January of 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. And I'm interviewing the Reverend Dr. Isaac Levy. We're in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Isaac Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the name that you were born with as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born with it a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your date-- what is your date of birth and your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14th of September, 1910. I'm in my 86th year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were your born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me something about your early childhood and family background?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents came from Poland, probably at-- about the turn of the century. All the family, except one, were born in this country. And that's my sisters and brothers. My eldest sister was born in Poland, came over here as a child. But the rest were all born in England. And I was educated in England both at school and at London University. So entirely English, but not blue-blooded.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your sort of Jewish education and, and life as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a normal Orthodox background. My father was a very simple master tailor, but deeply engrossed in his Judaism because we were all trained in that way. And I started off by an ordinary school and then left to go to a yeshiva in London. And from then on to Jews' College at the south seminary, where I graduated, and at the same time, graduated from London University.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at London University?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a-- we had quite a syllabus. We had to do four languages, mainly. There was English, German, Latin, and Greek. We did a classical background-- and then went on to do Semitics, that was Hebrew, Aramaic, and Syriac. And that was intensive sort of Hebraic, Talmudic studies, at the Jews' College, where I graduated.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I obtained a position in the-- in the ministry, first of all, as an assistant, a student minister, and then after, my own congregation. And I went from one synagogue to another. And then that war broke out. And I volunteered for the army. And I was the first actual minister of this country to volunteer for service as a chaplain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, that experience, what, what was the sort of-- the background to you becoming a minister of religion? How did that evolve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think primarily my home background. And my studies sort of automatically led me into that field. One goes through a period of doubt. But ultimately, you convince yourself that that's what you want to do. And that's what I did. And, and I've been doing it ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your brothers or sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm the only one in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you come from a, a family with some-- where there had been ministers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not an-- not an academic family, at all. I was the only member of the family that had any sort of an academic training.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is that viewed in the family when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very favorably, naturally. Naturally. They were more than happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So coming more towards the time of the war, you said you were the first to, to enlist. What made you want to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very much involved with this whole problem of anti-Semitism in this country. The-- we have the, you know, the Mosley area-- Era, which was a very painful one. And I was very much involved in the, what I call, the anti-fascist activity. I clashed somewhat with the Board of Deputies, which was sort of the establishment. And their attitude was a defensive one. My attitude was, well, we've got to be aggressively anti-fascist. And I even spoke on public platforms in Hyde Park and elsewhere. I was very much involved in this. And, of course, I also was very conscious of the fact that we were getting refugees coming over from Central Europe in this country. And meeting them and knowing what was happening in Germany, one couldn't help but to be-- direct oneself into this field. And when war broke out, I immediately volunteered for service. And for the first three months of the war, I was not in uniform yet. But I used to go down to a big training camp in Aldershot in Hampshire, and used to help with the senior chaplain in his office. So I did three days work in the fields, three days work in the office, and one day, a Saturday, in my synagogue until I was commissioned. And then once being commissioned, I was given a-- my first posting. Again, was to a, a training camp where former refugees from Germany were serving in the army, a place called Richborough Camp. And I was with them for quite a while until I was sent overseas. In 1941, I went overseas to the Middle East. I was there for three years. I became senior chaplain for the Middle East forces, having a delightful parish stretching from Turkey to Tunisia. That covered Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, Transjordan, North Africa, right up to Tunisia. So it was quite an experience. And whilst there, of course-- 1941 to 1944-- we were getting news of what was happening in Europe, particularly with our Palestinian Jews. I had 25,000 Hebrew-speaking Palestinian Jews serving in the British Army. And many of them were-- had relatives in Europe, of course. So we were very conscious of, of what the situation was. And, and this-- the antagonism to Germany became very intense. And that is why so many of these Palestinian Jews were anxious to get into the battle, as it were. And they were very frustrated that they weren't sent into the field. Until the early part of 1944, when the independent Jewish Brigade was formed. And they went out-- trained in, in Africa, went over to Italy, and were involved in the last stages of the war in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again-- I just want to, to go back a bit. You got married in?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1937. How did your wife feel about you enlisting at that point-- and your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: The community didn't mind because it had-- it's a job that had to be done. And the army needed Jewish chaplains. My wife, well-- throughout the whole of our life, she's had to bear the brunt. She realized that it was-- it was not an easy thing to do, but had to be done. By the time I went overseas we had a little boy. And I didn't see much of him for quite a while. I was away for three years. And then, as my wife said, I, I was more away than at home. So I didn't see the early years of my son's career, as it were. But she, a faithful wife would realize that this had to be done. And she has borne the brunt of my anxieties-- as I shall have, of course, to tell you about my experiences in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write to her frequently between '41, '44-- as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is so faithful, she kept all my letters. And there are hundreds and hundreds, and hundreds of letters. It was one of the methods by which I maintained my sanity. I always said, people could come to the chaplain and pour out their sorrows and anxieties. But to whom does the chaplain talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I talked to my typewriter. And I kept a diary. And, at the same time, I wrote letters as often it was possible. And the post service was excellent, both from, from the Middle East and Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she kept all these letters. And I needed those letters because later on, as, as you will hear, when I had to-- when I reached the stage when I could record my experiences in Germany, I turned to my letters to find out-- to be reminded of some of those things that were happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for that, she was-- I am very grateful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of the most memorable experiences from '41 to '44, with that-- that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We had a lot of problems. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The problems mainly were my status. In this respect, I was British, I had a British commission, and I had 25,000 Hebrew-speaking Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because of the political frustrations that these men suffered, I was looked upon by them, by many of them, as being a representative of the-- of the establishment, to which they took great exception. The British, on the other hand, used to say I'm the spokesman of the Palestinians. So I was between the hammer and the anvil. But, on the other hand, I had excellent relations with some of the leading figures of Palestine, as it then was. Moshe Shertok who became Moshe Sharett, was a-- became a very close friend. And I could talk to him. And I had a few very old friends from, from my early days serving in the Jewish agency. So I had people to whom I could talk, particularly on the political issues. But we were faced with a number of problems because it was still the period of British mandate. And Jews wanted to fight Hitler. And they weren't given the chance. And the late Dr. Weizmann, for example, said he could raise a division of Jews, who were ready to serve. And the answer he got was, we haven't the means of equipping them. That was an excuse. And this is the sort of thing that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what was the reason-- what was the reason that they wouldn't allow Jews to fight?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was trying to maintain the balance between Jews and Arabs. You see, Arabs were supposed to serve as well. They were given the opportunity of serving. But they, unfortunately-- and it very often happened-- you'd give them a rifle, and they would disappear with the rifle. So they were trained with a broom handles. [LAUGHS] And-- but you see, we had an element of military training available through what was called the Haganah. And I'll give you a funny-- one funny story. In one training camp, one of the members of Haganah, he was told by his training sergeant how to use a rifle. So he told him how to lie on his tummy, and he'd press the rifle. Then he says, now, there's a target. Shoot. And he shoots and gets a bullseye.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, oh, beginners luck. Do it again. He did it twice. Then he did it three times. And he said, have you ever had training before? He said, yes, sergeant. He says, where? By correspondence.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] He wasn't going to tell him that, of course, he had been trained by the Haganah. They're sort of the Underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were the kind of boys we were dealing with. They were exceptional element-- and they're highly, highly gifted men of-- who spoke several languages, for example. And there were a lot of frustrations as far as the Army was concerned. But we got a measure of satisfaction knowing you're dealing with these boys. We went out with them to the Western Desert.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one experience I had, I was captured by the Germans. And for 24 hours, I was a prisoner of war. And I actually had the Star of David on my cap. And, and the German officer, in whose car I was driving, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't realize what was happening. And I had on my sleeve, I had the word 'chaplain.' Because we didn't have any uniform under, just an open shirt and shorts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I-- one says to the other in German-- didn't realize I could speak German. He said, what does this word, 'chaplain,' mean? And he said, ugh, 'Er ist ein Vater.' He is a pastor, a sort of second-class quality to it-- [LAUGHS] second-class citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, fortunately, under attack of British guns we managed to escape. So that was a little experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, if that hadn't happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have been a prisoner of war in a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for longer.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the tragedy was-- or the good thing was that when I go back to army headquarters [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I reported about my experience, I managed to intercept the message which would have been sent back to my wife saying, 'missing presumed.' But I managed to-- and I wrote her-- sent her a cable saying, 'Escaped capture.' And she didn't realize what I meant. Anyhow, that was an experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But fortunately, it was the Afrika Korps. And they were a much nicer set of people. The German officers I spoke to were-- I managed to speak to in English, without telling them that I understood German, they were really behaved very nicely. That was the Afrika Korps. That was Rommel, who wasn't a Hitlerite. So that was one experience. I could go on. But it's-- that's really not the point.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want background, you've got it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back. What happened in '44? How did-- how did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, having served abroad for three weeks-- three months-- three years, I was entitled to one week's leave per year. So I was given three weeks leave, during which time my chief said that he needed me to help out a certain place. So I really had a couple of weeks free. And then I was posted overseas to Europe. By then, of course, we had invaded Europe. And I didn't get back into Europe until about late September 1944. And by then we were already in France and Belgium. And army, army headquarters-- I was attached to the headquarters of British Liberation Army. The headquarters were in Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I reported to Brussels and then moved forward with the army into Holland. And from Holland, ultimately, we crossed the Rhine. At a most appropriate time during the Passover week, we crossed the Rhine into Germany. What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Holland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to tell you. This is-- may interest you. On my way out to Brussels, I passed through a place called Malines. And somebody told me that there was an unusual prison there, an empty prison. I went in to have a look at it. I noticed the graffiti on the walls of the prison. And I realized that they were people who had been interned by the Germans. The Star of David was inscribed into the wall, cut into the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I realized that something had happened, that Jews were involved. And then I went into-- to Holland. And there, I came across something which-- quite exceptional.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a vast camp which was clinically clean-- it had been cleaned of everything. And there was a caretaker chap. He only spoke Dutch. But somehow, we managed to converse. And he told me that it had been a form of concentration camp. And-- but it had been cleaned up. The Dutch had been very careful about these things. Their hatred of Germany was intense. But, as I was leaving the camp, I saw something on the floor. I picked it up. And it was a label of a Zyklon-B gas, gas canister. Unfortunately, I've mislaid it, or I would have shown it to you. I thought to myself, we've been hearing about the use of gas, Zyklon-B. And here was an example of it. And it shook me rigid.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the one thing, which I-- which I-- and not only I, but everybody-- felt was, we're going into Germany ultimately. What will we find? Will there be any Jews left in Germany? We've met Jews in Brussels, of course. I didn't meet any in Holland. But then in the South of Holland, there weren't any. They had managed to survive in, in Brussels, somehow. But would we find anything in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a few weeks after we entered-- crossed the Rhine that rumor reached us-- it was a rumor-- that somewhere in the province of Hanover there was a camp. And it was riddled with typhus. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, naturally, my first reaction was, let's go and see what's happening. One of my chaplains, Leslie Hardman, was attached to that corps which entered the camp. So I rushed over. I got there about the third day of the liberation. That was about the 18th of April, 1945. And I saw a vast set of barracks. It had been the Wehrmacht barracks, the German Army barracks. And there were people walking around. Some of them, I could hear, they were speaking Yiddish. And they looked pretty healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where is this typhus epidemic? Anyway, I got talking to them. And then they realized they-- first time they'd seen a Jewish officer wearing a Magen David on his cap.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, excitement was tremendous. And they crowded around me. And they produced a little cardboard in the shape of Magen David in blue and white, which they stuck on their heads or wherever they could, and began to march past us, as though there was a parade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was the sort of guest of honor. And I said to them, what-- how is it that you're here? Well, they said, we were the late arrivals. We'd been brief-- from one camp to another. But where is this death camp that they're talking about, this typhus? That's further down the road. Well, I say goodbye to them. I sort of more or less saying au revoir, auf wiedersehen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went down to this camp. There, I came across these barbed wires. British Army's officers-- chaps on, on, on guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went with other people, or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on my own. I was on my own. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I entered the camp. And the first thing I was-- they did to me was, they sprayed me with DDT on my sleeves, down my shirt, down my trousers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What for? Protection against lice. Typhus was carried by lice. My first experience as I entered the camp. I only described it-- as I wrote in my little book, and I describe my experiences-- I was entering Dante's Inferno.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the paths were bodies laying all over the place, people crawling on all fours. [SIGHS] I entered the camp. There were huts. The smell was unbearable. I don't know whether it was the smell of death, or whether it was the smell of filth. They were lying in their own filth. There were no facilities. There was no water. There was no washing facilities. There was no medical facilities. They were not only suffering from typhus, but starvation. They were human skeletons. And they were lying around in their huts. And you couldn't tell whether they were alive or dead, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until you heard or saw, a little bit of movement, and you realized that person was alive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I walked into this hell. And I thought to myself, what can you do for these people? I can't do anything. If I can only talk to them, perhaps, I could sort of bolster up their morale, let them think they're not forgotten. Offer them food? But the British soldiers, in their sympathy, offered them food. The kind of food they offered them killed them because they couldn't take it. They choked on the food. So all the goodwill in the world didn't work. What food were they offered?

speaker: interviewee
text: What does a soldier offer? He's got-- he's got bully beef. He's got biscuits, or something of that nature. They couldn't take it. And the army realized that the medical corps had to be called in. And I-- one evening, I went over to see some officers, what they call the Casualty Clearing station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was appalled to see them. They were sitting around, drinking heavily, almost at the verge of intoxication.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to them, chaps, what are you doing? And they said to me, Padre, we are doctors. We are supposed to save life. This is hopeless. What can we do for these people? They're too far gone. And to drown them of their sorrows, they drank, which is understandable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then, the army realized that something had done. The medical services were inadequate. So they sent over to England a, a, a group of medical students. And they thought that the idea would be that they would treat them like they did the Bengal Famine. And they produced some sort of mush. And that was useless. Then somebody thought, ah, intravenous-- we'll feed them intravenously. When these people saw a, a syringe, they thought, they're going to kill us. Because they heard rumors of what was happening about killing them by syringes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the medical corps was faced with a tremendous dilemma. And people were dying. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The death camp lasted six weeks before it was destroyed by flamethrowers. During that time, my colleague and I officiated the burial of 21,000 corpses. Now, you know, you can say 21,000 doesn't mean anything. But they dug these mass graves. And they-- have you seen pictures of it? They threw the corpses in. And that was too slow. So they shoveled them in with bulldozers to bury them as quickly as possible, 5,000 at a time in each-- in these, these mass graves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all we could do-- we'd stand there at the edge. And we recited the burial services briefly. And they shoved-- the bulldozers shoveled the earth and then covered them up. They were dying at the rate of several hundred a day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the only salvation was to save those who showed some sign of life. So they transferred them by ambulances into an upper camp, where there was a-- been a-- a large-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a-- sort of an officer's mess. They called it the 'roundhouse,' they called it. And they converted that into a hospital. It probably became the largest hospital in Europe. They transferred the bodies into an ambulance. And the ambulance-- and then the body was taken into a place where the bodies were washed and deloused. And the people who did it were not only a few nurses they managed to find, but some of the soldiers. And they gave themselves the title of the 'human laundry.'

speaker: interviewer
text: I met one of these chaps after the war. He was the barber around the corner where we were living. And he recognized me. And I said to him, what were you in? And he said I was in the human laundry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he describes it-- men or women, it didn't make any difference. They were such skeletons. It was a body with a thin layer of skin. And we washed them and then put clean clothes on them. And then they went into another ambulance, which was a clean-- clinically clean ambulance. And so the one ambulance was used for, for the, the dirty bodies, as it were, and the one for the clean ones. And ultimately, the bodies were removed. And the dead were buried. And those who had a sign of life were transferred into the hospital. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was faced with this problem. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What could I do? I would like to notify the-- that some of the people had survived. So I thought, there's only one way to do this. We'll draw up a list of survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll go around with, with a big sheet, get their name, their, their city of origin-- that's all I wanted to know. And I started it. And I realized it was a hopeless task. As I noted their name, they died on me. You talked to a man, and he died as you talked to him. This was a horrible situation. And it was shattering, shattering. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we were glad to see the back of this, this-- of this, this death camp, as we called it. As I said, they brought in flamethrowers. There's only one way, and that was to burn it to the ground, in the hope they will destroy the lice at the same time. Little did I realize that the lice were coming out of the ground after the place had been burned. So it was a hell camp when it was there. And it was a hell camp when it was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the-- of the burning-- of the flamethrowers experience at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wasn't there at the time that they-- but I heard that the-- the army brought in the flamethrowers. And they just burned the huts to the ground. They were wooden huts, after all. It was easy enough to burn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then came the question of, what are we going to do with the survivors? There's no guarantee that all those who were brought into the-- into the hospital would survive. So we had deaths there, too, but not as many.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I must say that the medical services were fantastic then. We had two or three British general hospitals were transferred. We had nurses. We had doctors, all under the command of the chief medical officer of Second Army, a man by the name of Brigadier Glyn Hughes. I must mention his name because to me, he was man of Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was so attached to this camp and to his people and to the survivors, that even after the war, he still maintained his contact with survivors, whether they were in England, whether in America, or were in Israel-- so much so that when he died at the age of I think it was 80 or 90-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he said that-- and his last words were that if he was to-- there was to be any form of service, he would want a special prayer for the survivors of Belsen. And if I were around, would I do it? And I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the service took place at the parish church in Melbourne. And the former assistant chaplain in general, with whom I didn't get on too well, he officiated. And it was a very moving occasion. And the order of service, and the little prayer I composed, I sent to Yad Vashem in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
